key_insight: Vacuum-deposited, in situ encapsulated polycrystalline croconic acid thin films retain strong ferroelectricity and deliver useful piezoelectric and pyroelectric energy-harvesting performance on flexible substrates.
engineering_relevance: This offers a low-temperature, solvent-free route to scalable, lead-free organic devices for multisource environmental energy harvesting.
method: Vacuum-sublimed croconic acid (CA) films were deposited onto Ar plasma-treated flexible substrates.
Films were stabilized by in situ encapsulation with an adamantane-based remote plasma polymer.
The CA layer was integrated into multilayer piezoelectric/pyroelectric device stacks with dielectric polymer layers.
evidence_signal: Piezoresponse force microscopy showed robust ferroelectric domains and low coercive fields, while device testing reached up to 37 µW m−2 and a pyroelectric coefficient of about 10 µC m−2 K−1.
watch_item: Macroscopic output depends strongly on film thickness and stack design, so performance may vary without careful process optimization.
paper_title: Polycrystalline ferroelectric croconic acid for multisource environmental energy harvesting
